item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is too simple and played far too straight for me to really enjoy it. I can think of two black hole SCPs in Series 1 alone that blow this one out of the water in terms of creativity and sink-teeth-into-ness, let alone the rest of the site. This is sorely in need of some meat on its bones.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could trim your nails with it if you were extremely careful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: since this thing would be able to just cut off your arm, you might think its dangerous. Well, i think its a risky way to cut your nails

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How bout shoving 682 inside? Let’s see if he can escape THAT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Throw 682 into it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a necropost, but I really want to note how funny it is that qntm ended up, over a year after this comment was made, a regular user with some of the most upvoted articles on the site.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired: Edits below have been made. All WikiWalk team edits may be reverted by the original author at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 72 hours if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So this Mini Event Horizon is, for lack of a better term, a ticking time bomb. If not stopped its likely to engulf the planet and beyond. It just so happens that there is a skip (SCP-1032) that acts as a timer for such events, literally counting down to the destruction of entities since its a clock. The destruction of the planet via a growing event horizon certainly fits that bill. Recommending the addition of a potential addendum.

Addendum: As of the time of writing it is of note that the hand on SCP-1032 designated SCP-1032-15 will achieve its "midnight event" on 09/04/2690, approximately the same date as SCP-094 will full engulf the Earth at its current rate of expansion.

This of course is just one suggestion, however, as there are numerous anomalies which may prove useful in containing SCP-094.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love world ending SCPs! 1+

item.title: XK-class scenario
item.content:encoded:
If 094 doubles in size every 31 years, than we have around 22 doublings before the total destruction of Earth, and this is even if the object does not begin doubling faster as described in the above thread. Thus, it is about 8 times more dangerous than 1548, especially considering that it will destroy 1548 before it would ever get anywhere near Earth. Are there any SCPs more dangerous than this one?

Creepy fact: 22*31 is 682. Think about that for a moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That makes more sense, lending to the sense of fear as opposed to redundancy. Even if that wasn't the original idea, it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think what's meant by that sentence is that the rate of acceleration will also accelerate. As in, it will double it's size every 30 years, then it will double it's size every 29 years, then 28, then 27, etc. At least, that's what I think it means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And that is assuming it does not grow even faster, which is an assumption that almost no one here makes.

…Who the hell would make such an assumption at all? If the thing doubles in size about every 31 years, than it's obviously growing faster, it's growth being exponential as is stated in the article itself. Hell, you could calculate almost exactly its size in X years with that info. Honestly, I'd recommend removing that last sentence because it feels very out of place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's quite a minor error, I think he might have meant to state light as a seperate thing from matter that's also affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but there's a glaring error I can't believe nobody has noticed yet. LIGHT ISN'T MATTER. It's energy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see quite a lot of potential in this, but unfortunately the writing style and format don't feel SCP—ish enough.

A few edits to make the article seem less emotional and more 'scientific', as well as further details and/or experimentation logs would probably turn my opinion around. For now, it's a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was discovered in Argentina, and it's not exactly movable, so they gotta work with what they got.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this in Argentina? And why is our (politicaly supresed) army involved in the containment of a SCP? I find it funny that our forces are seen as capable. I suggest that they delete the argentinian army of the contanment procedire and leave only SCP agents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: interesting, I'd love to see more experimentation about the Foundation trying to revert it's growth, maybe some strange and unexpected results…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hopefully they don't just pop out of a corresponding hole elsewhere…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
my hypothesis it is rip in our universe, and "The blackness" is the void between universes, I would have said that possibly something from another universe or ours crated that hole to escape into or out of our universe. but two things seem out of place 1 the hole didn't close up so what ever it was running from (if thats the case could easily follow it and more importunely 2 NOthing no sound could possibly travel thru the void so pro-haps something is attempting to merge our universe together?

eather way nicely done +1 for u

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought so. Actually because I remembered seeing a comment you made about the same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited. SCP objects are always, always, always referred to with the full three digits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should the note at the end of the article refer to it as "094" instead of "94," or does that not matter very much?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I prefer our other black hole, just because it has a picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done, with some slight modifications which I hope haven't twisted the numbers back out of whack. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My suggestion? Say that it was 20 cm when found, is about 160 cm now, and appears to double its size every 25 years (which would at the moment give it a growing rate of just a few millimeters a year - right now, 4.4 cm/year, if I'm not mistaken). These numbers are closer to your initial ones, and will give this thing a diameter of about 410 meters in 200 years - slightly sooner than your original numbers, but I think still distant enough to stay true to the original concept. And it makes what follows seem even more scary.

Or rather, choose numbers that are similar to the ones above, but are a bit less round to make it seem more realistic. Humans may like multiples of fives and tens, as they are easy to work with, but nature usually isn't as convenient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, you can't store this in a gas filled chamber. Random movement of the atmosphere within will eventually result in it all getting sucked in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…y'know, I'm not sure which is worse: that I screwed up the math in the first place, or that it took more than two years for someone to point out the error. I think I know what I did wrong though (I think I jumped straight from centimeters to meters), but that does totally screw up the conception I had for it. Dammit. Even if I upped the rate of increase to a centimeter a month, which would still be imperceptible to the naked eye, it would still only be 50 meters across after 300 years— unnerving, but still not quite an existential crisis.

Winterwind, I'm not quite sure how I want to deal with this. Geometric growth is certainly a possibility, but so are tweaking the constant growth, or just making it grow at an inconsistent, unpredictable rate. For the moment, I'm going to jigger it so at least the math works, and decide on a long-term solution later. I'm certainly open to suggestions, especially about what values to plug in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The math in this one seems way off. If it's growing about one millimeter per month, then, assuming no acceleration, in three hundred years it will not have grown to be "half a kilometer wide", as the article states, but merely increased its diameter to just about 5 meters (which leads me to believe somewhere in the original calculation two additional zeros have slipped in).

I'd suggest changing it to past measurements already showing an exponential growth, which is still slow, but the extrapolation of which indicates it will grow to a size in the order of hundreds to thousands of meters in size within a few centuries (I would do it myself, but I'm not sure whether that wouldn't qualify as the kind of major change that should be accepted by the original author first - my apologies, I just joined the site).

If desired, I'd volunteer to produce a plot that shows the past and extrapolated growth of that thing (though only after New Year's… I won't have time during the holidays).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: mmmmm, i totally forgot about this one. much likey, much likey. in its original state it seems the sort of thing you'd OBVIOUSLY try to dispose of 682 into….this though….i think it'd be worthwhile to include a note along the lines of "due to the potential for added mass to increase the volume of area covered at a higher rate, no self-replicating or regenerative SCP's are to be disposed of into SCP-094". who knows where the material is coming from for 682 to repair himself? can you imagine sticking him in and suddenly this thing starts growing at a rate of an inch a minute? great way for him to accomplish his goals!

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I've got some other ideas for some other experimentation, though I don't want to bloat the article up too much.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Ok, sounds fair. I just mentioned it because it seemed an obvious experiment to try, not because it really bothered me or anything. Your revision, by the way, did a good job of saving the article. I'm looking forward to seeing what else you do with it.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… you have a point, cadence. Building a sealed container that could hold in a near-vacuum would be easy enough, even with the built-in handicap of having to work around 094. The main issues I can think of would be that (1) the container would have to hold up for many months to get any conclusive evidence, and (2) opening the container holding a near-vacuum could be hazardous to anyone or anything nearby. Not major problems though. For the moment, I'll say, they tried it and it didn't change anything, and rather than dealing with the potential implosion hazard, they made it gas-permeable.

ieverin, I'm sure there are a bunch of hypotheses about where 094 came from, but not a lot of testing of anything not directly related to How Do We Make It Stop Growing.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: I think another hypothesis would be that someone, either human or from anywhere else, put it there.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Well, the maximum pressure exerted on the structure (if the interior was a perfect vacuum and the exterior was normal atmosphere) would be 1 atmosphere; a structure to withstand that sort of pressure would be easy to construct.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: A valid hypothesis, but I made it gas-permeable because (in my head) they had originally tried a sealed container, but as more and more gas molecules fell into 094 via Brownian motion, the pressure inside the container dropped until it finally imploded. I might add that to the article at some point.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
I noticed that the containment enclosure is gas permeable.

Hypothesis: The "event horizon" grows as a result of gas molecules being absorbed and added to the object's volume.

Reinforcing the enclosure to being airtight will isolate the singularity and essentially stop its growth.

Seems like a reasonable precaution for researchers to take. Might even backfire on them, if whatever is on the other side doesn't appreciate being deprived of air.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
Yes, yes it would. And that's why the Foundation is pouring truckloads of resources into stopping or reversing this thing, or coming up with some contingency (though running away is not really an option— first Terra is swallowed, then the rest of the inner solar system, and then once Sol is gone, there's no gravity well to bind the rest of the solar system together).

In any case, here's the revised article. It still doesn't feel done to me, but hopefully it's better, at least enough to fend off deletion.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
That seems uncontainable though. I mean, a growing "black hole" is a pretty big deal, and would eventually lead to the end of the world no matter what.

I would suggest expanding upon where it leads, but I agree with Circeus; it'd be hard to come up with something really original.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: OK, thanks. Instead of making it Euclid, now I'm thinking of making it smaller but growing, slowly and inexorably. At the very least make it earn its Keter classification.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Take your best shot at it. Really, the worst that happens is that it ends up deleted anyway…you have nowhere to go but up.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: We already have too much "areas wot make stuff vanish". To salvage this would require superhuman imagination IMHO.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Original author unknown, and rewritten by Dave Rapp, who apparently hasn't been around in months. I'll adopt this one if no one has any objections, and beyond making it Euclid, I'm open to suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This entry has been reviewed and slated for editing. If you are the original author, please review this entry and comments, and see what changes could be made. Other users may edit ONLY:

  1. If the original author cannot be found/contacted
  2. If the original author gives permission

If no editing has been done on this entry after a period of two weeks, this entry will come under review for deletion. If you have any questions, please contact a mod or admin.

Start Date: 9/11/09
End Date: 9/25/09

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Or watch the Psi Factor episode that had pretty much the same theme.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
It would be cleanly severed as soon as you stuck it in.

For further reading on this subject, consider this short story by Sam Hughes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since this thing has no gravity, what would happen is someone stuck his arm in and tried to pull it out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like it could be useful for getting rid of some of the more problematic SCP's.
